sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 17-year-old non-binary individual, presents for evaluation of newly diagnosed hypercholesterolemia.
They were informed of the elevated cholesterol level after a routine annual checkup, where a finger prick test revealed a cholesterol level of 210.
They are worried about the finding and have been told to make lifestyle changes.
The patient is active, enjoys riding their bicycle, and tries to eat a relatively clean diet, but has a weakness for chocolate chip cookies.
Past medical history: The patient has no significant past medical history, but their grandparents reported that they did not have high cholesterol at a young age.
The patient's grandmother mentioned that a cousin died of a heart attack at age 44 or 45.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling healthy and has not experienced any issues with growing pains or other symptoms.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: The patient's blood pressure is within a normal range, but the specific values are not mentioned.

Physical exam: The patient's physical exam is unremarkable, with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops on the heart exam, clear lungs, no thyroid megalogy, and no hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on the abdominal exam.
Test Results: The patient's cholesterol level was elevated at 210.
The doctor reviewed the results and plans to order further tests, including a full cholesterol panel, glucose, complete metabolic panel, thyroid studies, and liver panels, as well as genetic testing.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has elevated cholesterol, which is a risk factor for premature heart attacks.
Problem list: Hypercholesterolemia; Suspected familial hypercholesterolemia.
Plan:
I ordered a full cholesterol panel, glucose, complete metabolic panel, thyroid studies, and liver panels to investigate the cause of the elevated cholesterol.
I also recommended genetic testing to determine if the problem is familial or secondary.
The patient will meet with a nutritionist to learn about a well-balanced diet, low in saturated fat and sugars.
They will continue to be active and exercise regularly, including bike riding.
I will follow up with the patient in 3-6 months to check their cholesterol level after they have made dietary changes.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen again in 3-6 months to check their cholesterol level after they have met with the nutritionist and made dietary changes.
